sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a male, reports a history of a right knee injury that occurred approximately three days ago while playing soccer, specifically trying to kick a ball and feeling a pop in the knee.
He describes the knee as swollen, with some of the swelling having resolved, but still experiences pain and limited function.
He rates the pain as a 6-7 out of 10, with some relief achieved with ibuprofen.
He denies any fever or other symptoms.
He reports being able to bend the knee but with pain at the end of the motion, and can straighten it but with difficulty.
He is able to bear weight on the knee but describes it as feeling loose and not normal.
Past medical history:
The patient does not mention any past medical history, social history, smoking, alcohol, or drug use.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any other symptoms in other organs.
Current medications:
The patient is currently taking ibuprofen for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The patient has a marked limp on the right leg upon ambulation.
There is significant effusion in the right knee with a slight bit of ecchymosis.
Palpation at the medial joint line is painful.
The patient has difficulty with passive range of motion, lacking a few degrees of terminal extension to 115 degrees and 20-30 degrees of terminal flexion, secondary to pain.
The patient has a positive Lachman exam.
Neurovascularly, the bilateral lower extremities are intact with muscle strength of 5/5.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a right knee injury suggestive of an anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) tear.
Problem list: Anterior cruciate ligament tear; [Status: confirmed]
Plan:
I ordered an MRI to evaluate the integrity of the ACL.
I prescribed meloxicam 50mg daily to reduce swelling and pain.
I recommended the patient to ice the knee if necessary, wear a knee immobilizer, and use crutches to avoid putting too much stress and strain on the knee.
I advised the patient to limit work activities, particularly sitting for long periods, and to do some slight activities to move the leg to avoid locking up.
I also advised the patient to avoid jogging until the results of the MRI are known.
Follow-up:
The patient will be scheduled for an MRI and I will review the results with them.
